or **Principality of Liège**[[2]](#cite_note-2) was a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [ecclesiastical principality](/wiki/Hochstift "Hochstift") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") that was situated for the most part in present-day [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). It was an [Imperial Estate](/wiki/Imperial_State "Imperial State"), so the [bishop of Liège](/wiki/Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Bishop of Liège"), as its prince, had a seat and a vote in the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Prince-Bishopric of Liège should not be confused with the [Diocese of Liège](/wiki/Diocese_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Diocese of Liège"), which was larger and over which the prince-bishop exercised only the usual responsibilities of a bishop.

The **Prince-Bishopric of Liège** or **Principality of Liège**[[2]](#cite_note-2) was a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [ecclesiastical principality](/wiki/Hochstift "Hochstift") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") that was situated for the most part in present-day [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). It was an [Imperial Estate](/wiki/Imperial_State "Imperial State"), so the [bishop of Liège](/wiki/Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Bishop of Liège"), as its prince, had a seat and a vote in the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/Imperial_Diet_(Holy_Roman_Empire) "Imperial Diet (Holy Roman Empire)").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Prince-Bishopric of Liège should not be confused with the [Diocese of Liège](/wiki/Diocese_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Diocese of Liège"), which was larger and over which the prince-bishop exercised only the usual responsibilities of a bishop.

The bishops of [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") acquired their status as [prince-bishops](/wiki/Prince-bishop "Prince-bishop") between 980 and 985 when Bishop [Notker of Liège](/wiki/Notker_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Notker of Liège"), who had been the bishop since 972, received secular control of the [County of Huy](/wiki/County_of_Huy "County of Huy") from [Emperor Otto II](/wiki/Emperor_Otto_II "Emperor Otto II").

From 1500, the prince-bishopric belonged to the [Lower Rhenish–Westphalian Circle](/wiki/Lower_Rhenish%E2%80%93Westphalian_Circle "Lower Rhenish–Westphalian Circle"). Its territory included most of the present Belgian provinces of [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_(province) "Liège (province)") and [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"), and some exclaves in other parts of Belgium and the Netherlands.

The ecclesiastical state briefly became a republic (the [Republic of Liège](/wiki/Republic_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Republic of Liège")) from 1789 to 1791, before reverting to a prince-bishopric in 1791. The role of the bishop as prince permanently ended when the state was annexed by France in 1795. In 1815 the territories it had held became part of the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands"), and in 1830 they were within the part of that kingdom which split off to become [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").

The principality ruled by the bishops of Liège was never part of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") or the Spanish and Austrian [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"), but from the 16th century onwards its politics were strongly influenced by the [dukes of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy") and later the [Habsburgs](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg").

In 1559 its 1,636 [parishes](/wiki/Parish "Parish") were grouped into eight [archdeaconries](/wiki/Archdeacon "Archdeacon"), and twenty-eight councils, **chrétientés** ([deaneries](/wiki/Deanery "Deanery")). The most important cities (**bonnes villes**) of the bishopric were: Liège, [Beringen](/wiki/Beringen,_Belgium "Beringen, Belgium"), [Bilzen](/wiki/Bilzen "Bilzen"), [Borgloon](/wiki/Borgloon "Borgloon"), [Bree](/wiki/Bree,_Belgium "Bree, Belgium"), [Châtelet](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2telet,_Belgium "Châtelet, Belgium"), [Ciney](/wiki/Ciney "Ciney"), [Couvin](/wiki/Couvin "Couvin"), [Dinant](/wiki/Dinant "Dinant"), [Fosses-la-Ville](/wiki/Fosses-la-Ville "Fosses-la-Ville"), [Hamont](/wiki/Hamont-Achel "Hamont-Achel"), [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt"), [Herk-de-Stad](/wiki/Herk-de-Stad "Herk-de-Stad"), [Huy](/wiki/Huy "Huy"), [Maaseik](/wiki/Maaseik "Maaseik"), [Peer](/wiki/Peer,_Belgium "Peer, Belgium"), [Sint-Truiden](/wiki/Sint-Truiden "Sint-Truiden"), [Stokkem](/wiki/Dilsen-Stokkem "Dilsen-Stokkem"), [Thuin](/wiki/Thuin "Thuin"), [Tongeren](/wiki/Tongeren "Tongeren"), [Verviers](/wiki/Verviers "Verviers"), [Visé](/wiki/Vis%C3%A9 "Visé") and [Waremme](/wiki/Waremme "Waremme").

The city of [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht") fell under the [joint jurisdiction](/wiki/Condominium_(international_law) "Condominium (international law)") of the prince-bishop of Liège and the [duke of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") (later the [States-General of the United Provinces](/wiki/States-General_of_the_Netherlands "States-General of the Netherlands")). The second city of the prince-bishopric thus kept its special status throughout the **ancien régime**.

## Medieval prince-bishopric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medieval prince-bishopric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png/250px-Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png)](/wiki/File:Belgian_Medieval_Dioceses.png)

This map shows the pre-1559 medieval [Diocese of Liège](/wiki/Diocese_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Diocese of Liège") (in green) which evolved from the **Civitas Tungrorum** and probably had similar boundaries.

The large diocese of the medieval bishops was, until 1559, much larger than the princedom which was in their possession. However, the princely domain was gradually enlarged by donations and by acquisitions. In the 10th century, the bishops received [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") lordship over the [county of Huy](/wiki/County_of_Huy "County of Huy"), which lay within the [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese"). Bishop [Notker](/wiki/Notker_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Notker of Liège") (972–1008) and his successors are thus referred to as princes within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). This situation continued until the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), and throughout that period of nearly eight centuries the Prince-Bishopric of Liège succeeded in maintaining a level of autonomy, though it remained under the emperor. This virtual independence was owed largely to the ability of its bishops, who on several occasions played an important part in international politics, being strategically positioned between [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Throughout the Middle Ages, the prince-bishopric was further expanded with the [lordship of Bouillon](/wiki/Lords_of_Bouillon "Lords of Bouillon") in 1096 (ceded to France in 1678), the acquisition of the [county of Loon](/wiki/County_of_Loon "County of Loon") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Looz*) in 1366 and the [county of Horne](/wiki/County_of_Horne "County of Horne") in 1568.

Notker, the founder of the principality, also rebuilt the cathedral of St Lambert, as well as the episcopal palace. He was also involved in other building activities in the city, which flourished under his rule (churches of St Paul, St. John the Evangelist, Sainte-Croix and St Denis). This bishop also strengthened the parochial organization of the city. He was one of the first church leaders to spread the observance of [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day"), which he authorized for his diocese. Under Notker's administration, following up on the work of Heraclius, educational institutions in Liège flourished. With these two bishops (and [Wazo](/wiki/Wazo "Wazo")) "The schools of Liège were, in fact, at that time one of the brightest literary foci of the period". In the 11th century the city was indeed known as the *Athens of the North*. "Liège for more than a century occupied among the nations a position in regard to science which it has never recovered". Subsequent bishops, Balderic of Looz (1008–1018), [Wolbodo](/wiki/Wolbodo "Wolbodo") (1018–1021), Durandus (1021–1025), Reginard (1025–1038), Nitard (1038–1042), the learned [Wazo](/wiki/Wazo "Wazo"), and Theoduin (1048–1075), valiantly sustained the heritage of Notker. The schools formed many brilliant scholars, and gave the Catholic Church popes [Stephen IX](/wiki/Pope_Stephen_IX "Pope Stephen IX") and [Nicholas II](/wiki/Pope_Nicholas_II "Pope Nicholas II"). The diocese also supplied the [University of Paris](/wiki/University_of_Paris "University of Paris") with a number of important doctors — [William of Saint-Thierry](/wiki/William_of_Saint-Thierry "William of Saint-Thierry"), [Gerard of Liège](/w/index.php?title=Gerard_of_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gerard of Liège (page does not exist)") and [Godfrey of Fontaines](/wiki/Godfrey_of_Fontaines "Godfrey of Fontaines"). [Alger of Liège](/wiki/Alger_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Alger of Liège") (1055–1131) was an important intellectual of the period. He was first appointed deacon of [church of St Bartholomew](/wiki/St_Bartholomew%27s_Church,_Li%C3%A8ge "St Bartholomew's Church, Liège") and finally retired at the monastery of [Cluny](/wiki/Cluny "Cluny").

In the reign of [Henry of Verdun](/wiki/Henry_of_Verdun "Henry of Verdun") (1075–1091) a tribunal was instituted (**tribunal de la paix**) to prevent war and enforce the [Peace of God](/wiki/Peace_of_God "Peace of God"). [Otbert](/wiki/Otbert_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Otbert of Liège") (1091–1119) increased the territory of the principality by purchasing the [Lordship of Bouillon](/wiki/Lords_of_Bouillon "Lords of Bouillon"). He remained faithful to emperor [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry IV, Holy Roman Emperor"), who died as his guest. Henry of Namur (1119–1121) was venerated as a martyr. During the administration of [Alexander of Juliers](/wiki/Alexander_of_Juliers "Alexander of Juliers") (1128–1134) the [pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), the [emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") and [St Bernard of Clairvaux](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux") visited Liège. The episcopate of [Raoul of Zachringen](/wiki/Raoul_of_Zachringen "Raoul of Zachringen") was marked by the preaching of the reformer [Lambert le Bègue](/wiki/Lambert_le_B%C3%A8gue "Lambert le Bègue"), who is credited with founding the [Béguines](/wiki/B%C3%A9guine "Béguine").

[Albert of Louvain](/wiki/Albert_of_Louvain "Albert of Louvain") was elected Bishop of Liège in 1191, but [Emperor Henry VI](/wiki/Emperor_Henry_VI "Emperor Henry VI"), on the pretext that the election was doubtful, gave the see to Lothair of Hochstadt. Albero's election was confirmed by the pope but in 1192, shortly after he took office, he was assassinated by three German knights at [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims"). It is probable that the emperor was privy to this murder but Albero was canonized. In 1195, Albert de Cuyck (1195–1200) formally recognized the [political franchise](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") of the people of Liège. During the 12th century, the cathedral [chapter](/wiki/Chapter_(religion) "Chapter (religion)"), along with the bishop, assumed a more important role in the history of the principality.

The struggles between the upper and lower classes, in which the prince-bishops frequently intervened, developed through the 13th and 14th centuries, and culminate in the 15th century in the pillage and destruction of the episcopal city. In the reign of Robert of Thourotte (1240–1246), [Saint Juliana](/wiki/Juliana_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Juliana of Liège") — a nun of [Cornillon Abbey](/wiki/Cornillon_Abbey "Cornillon Abbey") — was led by certain visions to the project of having a feast established in honour of the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament"). After much hesitation, the bishop approved of her idea but death prevented the institution of the feast. The completion of the work was left to a former [prior](/wiki/Prior_(ecclesiastical) "Prior (ecclesiastical)") of the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") in Liège, [Hugh of Saint-Cher](/wiki/Hugh_of_Saint-Cher "Hugh of Saint-Cher"), who returned to the city as papal legate. In 1252 Hugh made the feast of the [Blessed Sacrament](/wiki/Blessed_Sacrament "Blessed Sacrament") an obligation throughout his diocese. John of Troyes, who, after having been archdeacon at Liège, was elected pope as [Urban IV](/wiki/Pope_Urban_IV "Pope Urban IV"), encouraged the observance of the feast of [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)") in the whole Church. Another archdeacon of Liège became pope under the name [Gregory X](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_X "Pope Gregory X") and deposed the unworthy Henry of [Gueldres](/wiki/Gueldres "Gueldres") (1247–1274). The [Peace of Fexhe](/w/index.php?title=Peace_of_Fexhe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peace of Fexhe (page does not exist)"), signed in 1316 during the reign of [Adolph II de la Marck](/wiki/Adolph_II_de_la_Marck_(Bishop) "Adolph II de la Marck (Bishop)") (1313–1344), regulated the relations between the prince-bishop and his subjects. Nevertheless, internal discord continued and the episcopate of Arnold of [Horne](/wiki/County_of_Horne "County of Horne") (1378–1389) was marked by the triumph of the popular party. In 1366, the [county of Loon](/wiki/County_of_Loon "County of Loon") was annexed to the bishopric.

## Burgundian and Habsburg influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Burgundian and Habsburg influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Court_concert_at_Li%C3%A8ge.jpg/250px-Court_concert_at_Li%C3%A8ge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Court_concert_at_Li%C3%A8ge.jpg)

Prince-Bishop Johann Theodor of Bavaria at a court concert at Liège

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Het_paleis_van_de_prins-bisschoppen.jpg/250px-Het_paleis_van_de_prins-bisschoppen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Het_paleis_van_de_prins-bisschoppen.jpg)

The episcopal Palace at Liège

Upon the death of [Louis of Male](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Flanders "Louis II of Flanders"), [count of Flanders](/wiki/Count_of_Flanders "Count of Flanders"), in 1384, the Low Countries began their unification within the [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands"). Though the principality was still nominally independent, the [Dukes of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy") have had an increasing influence on its government. [Louis of Bourbon](/wiki/Louis_de_Bourbon,_Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Louis de Bourbon, Bishop of Liège") (1456–1482) was placed on the throne of Liège by the political machinations of [Philip the Good, Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Philip_the_Good,_Duke_of_Burgundy "Philip the Good, Duke of Burgundy"). The population resisted Burgundian rule leading to the [Liège Wars](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Wars "Liège Wars"), the destruction of [Dinant](/wiki/Dinant "Dinant") in 1466, and of Liège in 1468 by [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_the_Bold "Charles the Bold"), marking the ending of democratic ascendancy in the principality.

[Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") completed the union of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") in the 1540s, and unofficially also controlled the principality.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He nominated [Érard de La Marck](/wiki/%C3%89rard_de_La_Marck "Érard de La Marck") (1505–1538) who brought a period of restoration. Erard was an enlightened protector of the arts. It was he who commenced the struggle against the [Protestant reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), which his successors carried forth, especially Gerard of Groesbeeck (1564–1580). With the object of assisting in this struggle, [Paul IV](/wiki/Pope_Paul_IV "Pope Paul IV"), by [Bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") (**[Super Universi](/w/index.php?title=Super_Universi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Super Universi (page does not exist)")**, 12 May 1559), created new bishoprics in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"). The new bishoprics were created largely at the expense of the diocese of Liège; many of its parishes were given to the new dioceses of [Roermond](/wiki/Diocese_of_Roermond "Diocese of Roermond"), ['s-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/Diocese_of_Bois-le-Duc "Diocese of Bois-le-Duc"), [Namur](/wiki/Diocese_of_Namur "Diocese of Namur"), [Mechelen](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Mechelen-Brussels "Archbishopric of Mechelen-Brussels") and [Antwerp](/wiki/Diocese_of_Antwerp "Diocese of Antwerp"). The number of deaneries in the diocese of Liège was reduced to 13.

Liège formed the last link in the chain of Habsburg allies that made up the so-called [Spanish Road](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road"), a military corridor between Spanish-controlled [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") and the [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands"). Completely encircled by Spanish territory, Liège was protected by treaties of neutrality which permitted the passage of Spanish troops through the prince-bishop's territory provided that they spent no more than two nights in one place. The importance of the prince-bishopric to Habsburg military logistics in the [Eighty Years War](/wiki/Eighty_Years_War "Eighty Years War") prompted Spanish intervention foiling a Dutch invasion in 1595.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Most of the bishops in the 17th century were foreigners, many of them holding several bishoprics at once. Their frequent absences gave free scope for those feuds of the [Chiroux](/w/index.php?title=Chiroux&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chiroux (page does not exist)") and the [Grignoux](/w/index.php?title=Grignoux&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grignoux (page does not exist)") to which [Maximilian Henry of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_Henry_of_Bavaria "Maximilian Henry of Bavaria") ([archbishop of Cologne](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Cologne "Archbishop of Cologne"), 1650–1688) put a stop by the Edict of 1681. In the middle of the 18th century the ideas of the French [Encyclopedists](/wiki/Encyclopedists "Encyclopedists") began to be received at Liège; Bishop [de Velbrück](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Charles_de_Velbr%C3%BCck "François-Charles de Velbrück") (1772–1784), encouraged their propagation and thus prepared the way for the 1789 [Liège Revolution](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Revolution "Liège Revolution"). Partially connected with the French Revolution, a protest against the absolutist rule of prince bishop [César-Constantin-François de Hoensbroeck](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar-Constantin-Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Hoensbroeck "César-Constantin-François de Hoensbroeck") developed into the Liège Revolution. At the beginning of 1791, the revolution was crushed by troops on the orders of the Holy Roman Empire.

The prince-bishopric was dissolved in 1795, when it was annexed by [France](/wiki/France "France"). Its territory was divided amongst the départements of [Meuse-Inférieure](/wiki/Meuse-Inf%C3%A9rieure "Meuse-Inférieure"), [Ourthe](/wiki/Ourthe_(d%C3%A9partement) "Ourthe (département)"), and [Sambre-et-Meuse](/wiki/Sambre-et-Meuse "Sambre-et-Meuse").
